cue: Trace the loop, counting steps as the two indices close in.
Two pointers from both ends, swap and step inward.
prompt: Write `reverse(a)` that reverses the array `a` in place and returns it. Use two pointers; do not use the built-in `reverse`.
note: `[a[lo], a[hi]] = [a[hi], a[lo]]` swaps two array slots in one statement, with no temporary variable.

concept: reverse bug: looping the full length
cue: Follow each swap; watch what happens once the index passes the midpoint.
note: Looping the whole length swaps each pair twice, undoing itself; the pointers must stop at the middle.

cue: Two pointers from the ends, compare and return early on a mismatch.
prompt: Write `isPal(s)` that returns `true` if the string `s` reads the same forward and backward, else `false`. Use two pointers from the ends.

concept: palindrome bug: only one pointer moves
cue: Track both indices after each step; notice which one never changes.
note: With `hi` frozen, `s[hi]` stays 'a' while `lo` walks forward, so a real palindrome is rejected.

cue: Two pointers from the ends; shift based on the current sum versus the target.
prompt: Write `pairSum(a, t)` where `a` is sorted ascending. Return `[lo, hi]`, the indices of two entries that sum to `t`, or `null` if none. Use two pointers; do not use a hash map or nested loop.
note: On a sorted array this is O(n) time and O(1) space; a hash map matches the time but adds O(n) space.
mapsTo: LC 167 · Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted

cue: Follow each pointer step: the high end drops while the sum is too big, the low end rises while it is too small.
Trace until the pointers cross with no pair found.
Two pointers from the ends; advance the shorter wall each step.
prompt: Write `maxArea(h)` that returns the largest area `(hi - lo) * min(h[lo], h[hi])` over all pairs of walls in `h`. Use two pointers from the ends; do not check all pairs.
note: Advancing the shorter wall can't lose the best area: the width only shrinks, so keeping the shorter wall could never beat what you already measured.

cue: Track the running best area as the pointers move inward.
Two pointers at the ends; fill the result array from the back.
prompt: Write `sortedSquares(a)` where `a` is sorted ascending and may contain negatives. Return a new array of the squares in ascending order. Use two pointers from the ends; do not sort afterward.
note: The largest square sits at one of the two ends, so filling the result from the back is O(n) and skips a final sort's O(n log n).
mapsTo: LC 977 · Squares of a Sorted Array

cue: Trace which end's square is larger and where it lands in the result.
Track the write pointer, which advances only when an item is kept.
Read pointer scans; a lagging write pointer copies each new value.
prompt: Write `removeDupes(a)` where `a` is sorted ascending. Overwrite `a` in place so its first `k` entries are the distinct values in order, and return `k`. Use a read pointer and a lagging write pointer.
mapsTo: LC 26 · Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array

cue: Track the write pointer as repeated values are skipped.
Read pointer scans; a lagging write pointer keeps only non-matching entries.
prompt: Write `removeElement(a, val)` that removes every occurrence of `val` from `a` in place, keeping the order of the other entries. Return the count `k` of kept entries. Use a read pointer and a lagging write pointer.

cue: Compact the non-zeros with a lagging write pointer, then pad the tail.
syntax: while loop to fill remaining slots
prompt: Write `moveZeroes(a)` that moves every `0` in `a` to the end while keeping the order of the non-zero entries, mutating `a` in place and returning it. Use a read pointer and a lagging write pointer.

cue: Trace the non-zeros compacting forward, then the tail filling in.

concept: index array as a linked list
cue: Start at the given index and keep jumping to `nxt[cur]`; `-1` marks the end.
note: `nxt[i]` holds the index of the next node, so an array can encode a linked list without objects or pointers.

concept: one pointer twice as fast as the other
cue: Each step, slow follows one link and fast follows two; note how far apart they get.
note: On a cycle the fast pointer laps the slow one and they eventually land on the same index; on a straight list fast runs off the end first.

cue: Move a fast pointer two steps and a slow one; a meeting means a cycle.
prompt: Write `hasCycle(nxt, start)`. The list is an index array where `nxt[i]` is the next index and `-1` is null. Return `true` if following `nxt` from `start` reaches a cycle, else `false`. Use fast and slow pointers; do not use a set or array to mark visited nodes.
note: Floyd's tortoise and hare uses O(1) extra space; a visited set would cost O(n).

cue: Trace slow by one and fast by two until they meet or fast runs off the end.
Move fast two steps and slow one; when fast stops, slow sits on the middle.
prompt: Write `middleIndex(nxt, start)`. The list is an index array where `nxt[i]` is the next index and `-1` is null. Return the index of the middle node reached from `start`; for an even-length list return the second of the two middle nodes. Use fast and slow pointers.
mapsTo: LC 876 · Middle of the Linked List

cue: Consider how sort compares a negative and a multi-digit number when you give it no comparator.
note: Default sort compares the STRING form of each element, so '10' < '2' and '-1' < '-3'; pass `(x, y) => x - y` for numeric order.

cue: Sort, fix one element, then converge two pointers on the rest, skipping repeats.
prompt: Write `threeSum(a)` that returns all unique triples `[x, y, z]` from `a` with `x + y + z === 0`, each triple in ascending order and no duplicate triples. Sort first, then use two pointers; use a numeric comparator when sorting.
note: Sorting first makes equal values adjacent, so skipping neighbors that match dedups triples in O(1) extra space.

cue: Trace the fixed index plus the converging pair, skipping duplicate values.
